summary: Yegge uses an elaborate extended metaphor comparing Grand Theft Auto IV's storyline to corporate management training courses, satirizing the absurdity of mandatory workplace training videos. He draws parallels between in-game decisions (like executing an enemy) and real management decisions (like putting employees on performance improvement plans). The second half shifts to a love letter to classic video games, particularly Oblivion, and an argument that the industry should remake great old games rather than churning out mediocre new ones. Throughout, he weaves in references to his summer of gaming as an excuse for not blogging.
key_points: Corporate management training courses are absurdly formulaic, and GTA IV's moral choices are a better simulation of real management dilemmas than actual training videos
The 'passive-aggressive' management approach of having HR or someone else deliver bad news is analogous to restaurants having different waiters bring your food so yours doesn't get blamed
Truly great games only come along every few years, and the industry should remake classics with updated graphics and mechanics rather than releasing mediocre new titles
Game music is a critically underappreciated element that makes or breaks remakes and sequels
He's on a 'low-Microsoft diet' — avoiding Windows entirely in favor of Mac and Linux
money_quotes: I already knew how to react, for example, when my mafia boss told me he'd been betrayed by my previous manager, and I had to take him out. I'd already gone through stuff like that at Amazon dozens of times.
This playful enactment is obviously a metaphor for deciding whether to take the 'next step' in dealing with a problem employee: whether or not to put him on a Performance Improvement Plan.
Some managers feel it's better to have the employee mad at 'the company', since then the manager will then get better manager reviews from the employee.
Have you ever noticed that at restaurants, your waiter doesn't bring your food? Other waiters always bring out your food, during which time your waiter is nowhere to be seen.
Nearly 40 years as a gamer has convinced me that truly great games only come along every few years, and the rest are all just filler.
I'm on a low-Microsoft Diet, meaning I avoid Windows whenever I possibly can.
Once or twice a decade a truly extraordinary, groundbreaking game comes along.
It seems like it'd be better than releasing crap games that they know suck. Which is most of them.
key_insight: The absurd moral choices in GTA IV are barely distinguishable from the contrived scenarios in corporate management training, which says more about corporate culture than it does about video games.
